source.definition: to accept something as true or real, often without absolute proof

explanation.sourceText: The English verb "believe" comes from Old English "belȳfan", which originally meant "to hold dear or trust".
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „believe“ provine din engleza veche „belȳfan“, care inițial însemna „a ține aproape sau a avea încredere“.

source.additional: An irregular verb. Used to express conviction or trust in a statement or person.

target.definition: a fi convins de adevărul sau existența unui lucru

explanation.sourceText: Derived from the Latin "credere", meaning "to believe" or "to trust". The word has been preserved in Romanian and covers both religious and general meanings.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a crede“ provine din latinescul „credere“, însemnând „a crede“ sau „a avea încredere“.

target.additional: Regular verb of the 2nd conjugation (-e). Often used to express opinions or beliefs.

examples.sourceText: We believe that there is a solution.
examples.targetText: Noi credem că există o soluție.

examples.sourceText: They believe in a better future.
examples.targetText: Ei cred într-un viitor mai bun.

examples.sourceText: I have always believed in you.
He often believed what others said.
examples.targetText: El credea adesea ceea ce spuneau alții.

examples.sourceText: I would believe it if I had proof.
examples.targetText: Aș crede dacă aș avea o dovadă.

learningTips.text: The English word "believe" and Romanian "a crede" both express trust and conviction—imagine believing a friend’s story to remember it!
Think of "believe" in both everyday and religious contexts to grasp its versatile use.
Practice verb forms like "I believed" and "I will believe" to master Romanian conjugation.
Remember the phrase "believe in yourself" to connect the English and Romanian expressions.
